critic_raw:
The split uses stratified random sampling across all 5 years, holding out a random 15% as test. For cardiac event prediction with a 12-month follow-up window, patients recorded early in the 5-year period (year 1 recordings) have well-established outcomes, while patients from year 4–5 may be within or near the follow-up window—creating outcome ascertainment bias. If early-year patients with known outcomes are placed in test while later-year patients are in training, the model trains on higher-uncertainty labels and is evaluated on lower-uncertainty labels, artificially improving test performance. More fundamentally, random mixing of temporal observations means the model may train on ECG recordings from patients already known (in later training data) to have had cardiac events, leaking outcome information backward in time.

The comparison is fundamentally imbalanced in information access: the CNN receives raw ECG waveforms (12 leads × 1,250 samples = 15,000 data points per recording), while the logistic regression receives only static clinical features with zero ECG-derived information. This does not test 'whether CNN-based waveform analysis predicts better than LR on the same data'—it tests whether ECG data contains predictive information at all. A fair comparison would either give the LR extracted ECG features (QT interval, ST segment, heart rate variability) or test the CNN against a baseline that also receives ECG features in handcrafted form.

The validation set is described as 'year 4, ~2,000 patients.' This is a temporal split for the validation set, but the overall split is stated as stratified random sampling. These are inconsistent: if validation is year 4, the split is partially temporal; if overall sampling is random, year 4 patients should be distributed across all sets. The split procedure is self-contradictory and cannot be implemented consistently.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random sampling across 5 years with a 12-month follow-up window creates outcome ascertainment bias: early-year patients have stable outcomes while late-year patients may be within the follow-up window, and random mixing confounds temporal ordering with label reliability.

The CNN receives raw waveform data while the LR receives zero ECG-derived features; the comparison tests whether ECG data is predictive at all rather than whether CNN representations are better than handcrafted ECG features, making the hypothesis untested.

The split procedure is internally contradictory: overall sampling is described as stratified random, but the validation set is specifically defined as 'year 4 patients'—these two descriptions cannot be simultaneously satisfied.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random sampling across 5 years with a 12-month follow-up window creates outcome ascertainment bias: early-year patients have stable outcomes while late-year patients may be within the follow-up window, and random mixing confounds temporal ordering with label reliability.

The CNN receives raw waveform data while the LR receives zero ECG-derived features; the comparison tests whether ECG data is predictive at all rather than whether CNN representations are better than handcrafted ECG features, making the hypothesis untested.

The split procedure is internally contradictory: overall sampling is described as stratified random, but the validation set is specifically defined as 'year 4 patients'—these two descriptions cannot be simultaneously satisfied.
